name: cable squatting curl
instructions: Attach a cable handle to the lowest setting on a cable machine.
Stand facing the machine with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Hold the cable handle with an underhand grip, palms facing up, and arms fully extended.
Lower your body into a squat position, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes.
As you squat down, curl the cable handle towards your shoulders, keeping your elbows close to your sides.
Pause for a moment at the top of the curl, squeezing your biceps.
Slowly lower the cable handle back to the starting position, fully extending your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Attach a cable handle to the lowest setting on a cable machine. Stand facing the machine with your feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the cable handle with an underhand grip, palms facing up, and arms fully extended. Lower your body into a squat position, keeping your back straight and knees behind your toes. As you squat down, curl the cable handle towards your shoulders, keeping your elbows close to your sides. Pause for a moment at the top of the curl, squeezing your biceps. Slowly lower the cable handle back to the starting position, fully extending your arms. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
